nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, fast-flowing, highly oxygenated headwaters and minor tributaries with rocky substrate; cobble, pebble, and gravel beds covered in biofilm, diatoms, and aufwuchs; Pearl River system and adjacent drainages in southern China (Guangdong, Guangxi, Hunan, Jiangxi, Guizhou, Yunnan), Hong Kong, and northeastern Vietnam

decorPreferences: smooth water-worn rocks and flat stones for grazing and resting
strong flow from powerhead or wavemaker (10–15× turnover per hour)
strong lighting to promote algae and diatom growth on hard surfaces

diet.notes: Aufwuchs grazer: scrapes biofilm, diatoms, algae, and associated microorganisms from hard surfaces. In aquaria, supplement with algae wafers, spirulina tabs, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber), and sinking gel foods. Occasional frozen or live small invertebrates (bloodworm, Daphnia) accepted. Adequate biofilm on tank surfaces is essential; fish frequently starve in low-flow or newly set-up tanks that lack established algae growth.

goodWith: other hillstream loaches sharing similar flow and temperature requirements
small cool-water cyprinids (White Cloud Mountain Minnows, Danios)
small peaceful loaches tolerant of high flow
small rasboras at the cooler end of their range
Amano shrimp and larger ornamental shrimp in high-flow setups
avoidWith: warm-water tropical community fish (temperature mismatch above 25°C)
boisterous or fin-nipping species that outcompete for food
slow-water species requiring low flow (gouramis, bettas, fancy guppies)
Butterfly Hillstream Loach (Beaufortia kweichowensis, fw-fish-158) in small tanks — territorial competition likely between similar bottom-dwelling grazers

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Liniparhomaloptera disparis disparis — verify URL availability; standard FishBase contributed photo.

summary: The nominal subspecies of the Broken-Band Hillstream Loach, native to fast-flowing southern Chinese mountain streams. Demands cool water (20–24°C), very high flow, and mature biofilm-rich surfaces to graze constantly. Distinguished from the Hainan subspecies (L. d. qiongzhongensis) by its mainland southern China distribution.
careNotes:
Liniparhomaloptera disparis disparis is the nominal (nominate) subspecies of the Broken-Band Hillstream Loach, restricted to mainland southern China (Pearl River system and adjacent drainages: Guangdong, Guangxi, Yunnan, Guizhou, Hunan, Jiangxi), Hong Kong, and northeastern Vietnam. A second subspecies, L. d. qiongzhongensis, is endemic to Hainan Island and is distinguished by morphological differences in the lateral-line band pattern. In the aquarium trade the two subspecies are rarely differentiated and are sold collectively under the species name Liniparhomaloptera disparis.

MERGE NOTE: This entry (fw-fish-396) represents the nominal subspecies L. d. disparis. Entry fw-fish-287 (broken-band-hillstream-loach) covers the full species Liniparhomaloptera disparis and should be considered the primary record; these two entries are merge candidates. Subspecific distinction has limited practical relevance in the hobby.

Successful long-term care hinges on replicating the fast-moving rocky torrent habitat. Filtration should achieve 10–15× tank volume turnover per hour; a dedicated powerhead or wavemaker aimed across flat grazing stones is strongly recommended. High dissolved oxygen is non-negotiable — surface agitation must be vigorous. Temperature must be kept in the 20–24°C range; temperatures above 25°C increase disease susceptibility and reduce dissolved oxygen saturation. A biologically mature tank with established algae, diatom, and biofilm colonies on rocks and driftwood is essential before introducing this species; fish routinely starve in newly set-up or sterile tanks.

The species is a constant grazer. Supplement natural biofilm with algae wafers, spirulina tablets, and blanched vegetables placed directly on grazing rocks. Occasional small live or frozen invertebrates (bloodworm, Daphnia) are accepted and beneficial for conditioning. Keep in groups of four or more; lone specimens show less natural behaviour. A tight-fitting lid is essential — these loaches are capable climbers. Best kept in a hillstream biotope alongside cool-water companions (White Cloud Mountain Minnows, small danios) sharing the same flow and temperature requirements. Not suitable for standard warm-water community setups.
breedingNotes: Seasonal spawner; reproduction in the wild corresponds to the dry season in southern China and Hong Kong (approximately October–April). In captivity, conditioning with varied high-quality foods and a seasonal temperature drop may trigger spawning. Females are dominant and excavate shallow depressions in substrate or between stones as egg deposition sites. Eggs are approximately 1 mm in diameter, orangish in colour; they hatch in roughly 3 days at 22°C. Fry absorb yolk over a further 3–5 days before emerging at 6–8 mm total length. Sexual maturity is reached within one year. Breeding in the home aquarium is uncommon but has been reported in mature, well-maintained hillstream biotope setups.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Liniparhomaloptera disparis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided temperature (20–24°C), pH (6.5–8.0), hardness (36–268 ppm ≈ gH 2–15, kH 1–8), native range (Pearl River system, southern China provinces, Vietnam, Hong Kong, Hainan), habitat (fast-flowing oxygenated headwaters, rocky substrate), tank base dimensions (80×30 cm minimum), filter turnover (10–15×), diet (biofilm, algae, small invertebrates), group size (4+), breeding details (seasonal Oct–Apr, egg hatch ~3 days, fry 6–8 mm), subspecies notes (L. d. qiongzhongensis from Hainan). Profile covers the full species including the nominal subspecies.

additional.name: FishBase — Liniparhomaloptera disparis
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 8.0 cm TL), temperature range (22–24°C), distribution (Asia: China and Vietnam), habitat (fast-flowing bottom of mountain streams, demersal, freshwater), taxonomy (Gastromyzontidae, Cypriniformes), trophic level 2.7 (primary consumer). IUCN Least Concern (assessed 2024). Subspecies L. d. disparis confirmed as nominal subspecies from mainland southern China and Vietnam; L. d. qiongzhongensis from Hainan Island.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Hillstream Loach Care Guide
additional.notes: General hillstream loach guide applicable to Gastromyzontidae including Liniparhomaloptera. Confirmed temperature range 20–24°C, supplemental feeding with sinking wafers and blanched vegetables, group housing of 3–4+, tight-fitting lid requirement, and compatibility with peaceful cool-water community fish. Used for general husbandry corroboration.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Hillstream Loaches
additional.notes: Feature article covering hillstream loach genera including Gastromyzontidae members from southern China. Confirmed aufwuchs grazing specialisation, high oxygenation requirements, cool-water temperature demands, and the importance of a mature biologically active tank before introduction. Corroborates rheophilic lifestyle and starvation risk in low-flow setups.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Hillstream Loaches: The Torrent Surfers
additional.notes: Specialist article on hillstream loach natural history and aquarium husbandry. Confirms Gastromyzontidae family placement, fast-water torrent biotope, flat-body morphology as torrent adaptation, dietary specialisation for micro-aufwuchs, and seasonal breeding linked to dry-season water-level changes in southern Chinese drainages.

fish.breedingNotes: Seasonal spawner (Oct–Apr); females dominant and control egg deposition. Eggs hatch ~3 days at 22°C; fry emerge at 6–8 mm after yolk absorption. Sexual maturity within one year. Captive breeding uncommon; requires mature hillstream biotope and conditioning.
